title: Renewing or rupturing the sociology of education?
abstract: Click to increase image sizeClick to decrease image size Notes 1. NB, the Meyer here is Heinz‐Dieter Meyer, not John Meyer, the founding father of institutionalism in education and beyond. 2. I should point out that at the request of the publishers of the book, Teachers College Press, I supplied a sentence of endorsement for the back cover. It reads: ‘This book demonstrates the continually expanding range and depth of Carlos Torres' rich, vital unique and indispensable contribution to the sociology of education.’ Based on the quick reading of the text I was sent to do the endorsement, I decided that I would like to develop a rather more nuanced take on the book, and that it would fit very well into this review essay, which I had already agreed to do. 3. For which we are offered as a definition: ‘The State is a public power above both ruler and ruled which provides order and continuity to the polity’ (A. Vincent. 1987. Theories of the State, 218. Oxford: Basil Blackwell), which is an interesting definition, but one that seems to sit rather uneasily both with the neo‐Marxist tone of much of the discussion, and with what we know of the character of at least some of the states in Latin America that are referred to. The use of the definition could contribute considerably through further discussion, in terms of its origins and analytic consequences. Later, the dependent state is seen as shaped: by the nature of the peripheral role that its economy plays in the world system and the corresponding enormous influence that the dynamic of metropolitan capitalism has on its development process. The Third World state is also conditioned by the significant noncapitalist (postfeudal) elements in its own political system. (p.102) which is not immediately compatible with Vincent's definition.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
